text:
Steganography in the digital age is concerned with reducing a distortion function for efficient payload embedding. While efficient coding schemes such as Syndrome-Trellis Codes (STCs) give optimal solutions for the steganographic encoding problem, once a distortion function is chosen, the issue that persists is the design of the distortion function to limit as much as possible the statistical trace of the message on the covercarrier that a warden might try to analyse. In this paper, we propose the Universal Wavelet Relative Distortion (UNIWARD) distortion design that can be used for all imaging contexts including spatial, JPEG and side-informed steganography for JPEG.

We measure the embedding distortion in the UNIWARD framework by summing the relative coefficient changes in a directional wavelet filter bank decomposition. In fact, using directional high-pass filters, the distortion is concentrated to those areas of the cover that are hard to describe in multiple directions, i.e., to complex texture patterns and to highly contaminated noise; on the other hand, smooth surfaces and clean edges are assigned higher distortion costs in order to avoid revealing any noticeable effects from the steganalysis analysis. Experiments that use several state-of-the-art rich models and ensemble classifiers show that steganalyzers that are adapted to distortion measures provided by the UNIWARD framework or slight modifications of it are either matched or even outperformed by steganalyzers adapted to existing state-of-the-art specialized distortion measures. In summary, the versatile and universal approach provided by the UNIWARD framework for measuring distortion in steganography has been clearly validated in numerous diverse scenarios.
